headline: Cheltenham College Opens Its Kuala Lumpur Campus — Betting on Malaysia's Moment
dek: A direct franchise of one of Britain's best-known independent schools has taken its first students in the Malaysian capital, arriving just as the government launches a decade-long national education overhaul.
author: marcus-chen
heroAlt: Cheltenham College Opens Its Kuala Lumpur Campus — Betting on Malaysia's Moment

body.text: Cheltenham College International School Kuala Lumpur — trading as CCKL — welcomed its founding cohort of students in early 2026, making it the latest and arguably most prominent new British-franchise school to open in Southeast Asia this year. The school is a co-educational day school for pupils aged six to 18, offering a curriculum pathway through IGCSE and A Levels, and is operated under a direct partnership with Cheltenham College UK, which was founded in 1841 and holds an 'Excellent in All' rating from the Independent Schools Inspectorate.

The Kuala Lumpur campus is owned by Edumaax Education Group, a Malaysian education company chaired by Tunku Dato' Yaacob Khyra — himself an Old Cheltonian — and backed by MAA Group Bhd. Edumaax operates a portfolio of schools and higher education institutions across Malaysia, giving the new campus an established operator behind it at a point when many British franchise schools in Southeast Asia have stumbled on local execution.

CCKL's opening coincides with Malaysia's rollout of its National Education Plan 2026–2035, a ten-year government blueprint focused on strengthening critical thinking, holistic development, and global readiness. That policy backdrop has accelerated parental interest in internationally recognised curricula, with British and IB schools in the Klang Valley reporting strong inquiry volumes heading into the 2026–27 admissions cycle. Premium British schools in KL can expect to charge in the range of MYR 35,000 to MYR 130,000 annually depending on year group, a market that has grown significantly in the past three years.

The school's founding head of school, Paul Samuel, brings experience from British and international schools across Europe, North America, the Middle East, and India. The academic framework is built around what CCKL describes as five core principles — Curiosity, Creativity, Character, Community, and Continuous Excellence — drawn from Cheltenham College UK's educational philosophy and adapted for the Malaysian context.
A crowded market, but room at the top

Kuala Lumpur already has more than 100 international schools across the city and its surrounding districts, with British-curriculum operators including Nord Anglia's British International School of Kuala Lumpur among those well established at the premium end. CCKL's pitch is differentiation through heritage: Cheltenham College has links with Malaysia stretching back more than 50 years, and the franchise model gives the KL campus direct access to curriculum development, teacher training, and quality assurance from the UK parent.

Whether that is enough to capture meaningful market share in one of Asia's most competitive international school markets remains to be seen. What is clear is that Malaysia's expanding middle class and a steady expatriate inflow are sustaining demand that most operators describe as structurally robust, whatever the near-term economic noise. CCKL is inviting families to join its founding community ahead of the 2026–27 academic year.
